steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: P1 gave equal actual ratings to both styles. The graph shows correlation between model-predicted ratings and P1's actual ratings. The question asks what the model itself predicted about P1's ratings.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'P1 would derive more pleasure from impressionist than color-field': The graph shows a high correlation (0.58) for impressionist and a low one (0.14) for color-field. A high correlation means the model's impressionist predictions tracked P1's actual ratings closely; a low correlation means they diverged. That asymmetry doesn't establish which style the model rated higher — it just shows the model predicted something different for each. ✗
  • Choice B — 'P1's impressionist and color-field ratings would differ from one another': Since P1 actually rated both styles equally, the high impressionist correlation (0.58) vs. the low color-field correlation (0.14) can only be explained if the model predicted different ratings for the two styles — if the model had predicted equal ratings, the correlations would track more symmetrically. ✓
  • Choice C — 'P1 would derive less pleasure from impressionist than color-field': Same problem as A — the correlation gap doesn't tell us which style the model ranked higher, only that the model treated them differently. ✗
  • Choice D — 'P1's impressionist and color-field ratings would equal one another': If the model predicted equal ratings for both, the correlations would be similar, not 0.58 vs. 0.14. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
